sub_topics.explanation: Counting touching objects involves using tactile methods—such as placing fingers, tapping, or arranging physical items—to visually and physically track quantities. This hands-on approach strengthens numerical intuition by linking counting to direct, observable interactions with materials. It is ideal for early learners to build foundational number sense through concrete manipulation.
examples: Place 5 red pom-poms in a basket and ask: ‘If I remove two, how many will remain if I add three more?’
Use 8 wooden spoons and arrange them in a spiral on a tray, then count by touching each one to find the total if two are hidden under a cup.
Draw 15 dots on a large sheet of paper and ask: ‘If I connect every dot with a line, how many lines will you touch if you start at the first dot and move clockwise?’

sub_topics.explanation: The concept of joining groups involves combining distinct collections of objects into a single, larger group to determine the total count. It emphasizes the *part-to-whole* relationship, where the sum of individual group sizes equals the total size of the combined group. This sub-topic is foundational for understanding addition and real-world applications of combining quantities.
examples: Stand on one foot for 1 minute and count each second aloud while keeping your balance.
Do a deep knee bend 15 times in a row, counting each bend with your toes.
March in place while tapping your right foot 20 times, then switch to tapping the left foot for another 20 times.
